food-drink.name: Food & Drink
food-drink.title: Food & Drink Du'as: what to say before and after eating, in Arabic, transliteration and meaning
food-drink.meta_description: The sunnah du'as of food and drink: Bismillah before eating, alhamdulillah after, the du'a for your host, and the words for breaking a fast. Arabic, transliteration, meaning and source.
food-drink.keywords: dua before eating, dua after eating, bismillah before food, alhamdulillah after food, dua for the host, dua for breaking fast, iftar dua, Hisn al-Muslim, Buruja
lede: Every meal you have ever eaten was provision you did not create. The wheat, the water, the strength in your own hand to lift the food to your mouth: none of it was yours to begin with. The Prophet ﷺ taught us to bracket the table with remembrance, a name at the start and praise at the end, so that even an ordinary plate becomes an act of worship.
These are the words for eating and drinking: how to begin, how to give thanks, how to pray for the one who fed you, and what to say when you break a fast.
intro: Say Bismillah as you begin, and if you forget, say the words for that as soon as you remember. When you finish, give the praise. If someone has fed you, turn back to them with a du'a before you leave their table. None of it takes more than a breath.
These are everyday words. Keep the short ones on your tongue until they come without thinking, and let the meaning sit in your heart so that gratitude, not habit, is what is moving.
closing: To eat without a thought is to treat provision as something owed. To begin with His name and end with His praise is to eat as a guest at a table you did not set, honest about Who set it. The same hand that made the food made the body that needs it, and even your ability to be grateful is a gift on top of the gift.
O Allah, bless us in what You have provided, make us grateful for every mouthful, and feed those who have fed us. Let no meal pass our lips without Your name upon them.

faq.q: What do you say before eating?
faq.a: Say Bismillah, 'With the Name of Allah,' as you begin. The Prophet ﷺ taught that if you forget to say it at the start, then when you remember you say: Bismillahi fi awwalihi wa akhirih, 'With the Name of Allah, in the beginning and in the end.'

faq.q: What do you say after finishing a meal?
faq.a: Praise Allah for the food. A simple form is Alhamdulillahil-ladhi at'amani hadha wa razaqanihi, 'Praise is to Allah Who has given me this food and sustained me with it,' acknowledging that the provision and the strength to receive it both came from Him.

faq.q: What is the du'a for breaking a fast?
faq.a: When the fast is broken there is the du'a Dhahabaz-zama', 'The thirst is gone, the veins are moistened, and the reward is confirmed, if Allah wills.' There is also a du'a to ask before iftar, by Allah's mercy which encompasses all things, that He forgive you. Both are gathered here.

faq.q: How do you pray for someone who feeds you?
faq.a: Turn back to your host with a du'a before you leave. The Prophet ﷺ taught: Allahumma at'im man at'amani wasqi man saqani, 'O Allah, feed the one who has fed me and give drink to the one who has given me drink,' and to ask Allah to bless them, forgive them and have mercy on them.

email.subject: A name at the start, praise at the end
email.alt_subject: What the Prophet ﷺ said over every meal
email.preview: The du'as of the table: before eating, after, for your host, and for breaking a fast.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Every meal you have ever eaten was provision you did not create. The food, the water, even the strength in your hand to lift it: none of it was yours to begin with. The Prophet ﷺ taught us to bracket the table with remembrance, a name at the start and praise at the end, so that even an ordinary plate becomes worship.

We have gathered the du'as of food and drink, each with Arabic, a transliteration to help you learn it, the meaning and its source: how to begin with Bismillah, how to give thanks when you finish, how to pray for the one who fed you, and the words for breaking a fast. They are short. Keep the small ones on your tongue until they come without thinking.

To eat without a thought is to treat provision as something owed. To begin in His name and end in His praise is to eat as a guest at a table you did not set, honest about Who set it.

The full set, formatted to read or recite, is here:

social.hook:
Every meal you have ever eaten was provision you did not create.

The Prophet ﷺ never let one pass without a word.
social.caption:
The du'as of food and drink.

A name at the start, praise at the end: Bismillah before you eat, alhamdulillah when you finish, a du'a for the one who fed you, and the words for breaking a fast.

None of it takes more than a breath. To begin in His name and end in His praise is to eat as a guest at a table you did not set.

Save this, and read the full set with Arabic, transliteration and meaning at buruja.com/adhkar/food-drink

#DuaBeforeEating #DuaAfterEating #SunnahOfTheProphet #IftarDua #Dhikr #Dua #IslamicReminders #Buruja #Islam

food-drink.source_note: Prophetic adhkar transcribed verbatim from Hisn al-Muslim (Sa'eed al-Qahtani) via sunnah.com; al-Bukhari and Muslim taken as sahih, jayyid/reliable as hasan. Recitation audio: the official Hisn al-Muslim audio (hisnmuslim.com) for the adhkar. Selection follows Hisn al-Muslim, with thanks to lifewithallah.com; framing is Buruja's own. FOR SCHOLAR REVIEW: confirm every Arabic text, reference, repetition count and grading before publication.

items.title: When you break your fast
items.arabic: ذَهَـبَ الظَّمَـأُ، وَابْتَلَّـتِ العُـروق، وَثَبَـتَ الأجْـرُ إِنْ شـاءَ الله
items.translit: Dhahabaẓ-ẓama', wabtallati ‘l-`urūq, wa thabata ‘l-'ajru in shā Allāh.
items.en: The thirst is gone, the veins are moistened, and the reward is confirmed, if Allah wills.
items.note: Said as the fast is broken. The thirst gone, the body restored, and the reward, by Allah's leave, written down. A small line at sunset that gathers the whole day of fasting into a word of hope.

items.title: Asking forgiveness before iftar
items.arabic: اللّهُـمَّ إِنَّـي أَسْـأَلُـكَ بِرَحْمَـتِكَ الّتي وَسِـعَت كُلَّ شيء، أَنْ تَغْـفِرَ لي
items.translit: Allāhumma innī as'aluka bi raḥmatika ‘l-latī wasi`at kulla shay' an taghfira lī.
items.en: O Allah, I ask You by Your mercy, which encompasses all things, that You forgive me.
items.ref: Ibn Majah 1/557 from a supplication of Abdullah bin 'Amr
items.note: The hour of breaking the fast is a time when du'a is answered, so the fasting person asks Allah, by the mercy that encompasses all things, to forgive.

items.title: Saying Bismillah before eating
items.arabic: إِذَا أَكَلَ أَحَدُكُمْ طَعَاماً فَلْيَقُلْ بِسْمِ اللَّهِ، فَإِنْ نَسِيَ فِي أَوَّلِهِ فَلْيَقُلْ بسمِ اللَّهِ فِي أَوَّلِهِ وَآخِرِهِ
items.translit: When anyone of you begins eating, say:
items.en: When anyone of you begins eating, say: With the Name of Allah. And if you forget then, when you remember, say: With the Name of Allah, in the beginning, and in the end.
items.note: Begin with the Name of Allah, and if you forget at the start, say it as soon as you remember: in the beginning and in the end. The Prophet ﷺ taught that this keeps the food from being shared by Shaytan.

items.title: After food, and after milk
items.arabic: مَنْ أَطْعَمَهُ اللَّهُ الطَّعَامَ فَلْيَقُلْ: "اللَّهُمَّ بَارِكْ لَنَا فِيهِ وَأَطْعِمْنَا خَيْراً مِنْهُ"، وَمَنْ سَقَاهُ اللَّهُ لَبَناً فَلْيَقُلْ "اللَّهُمَّ بَارِكْ لَنَا فِيهِ وَزِدْنَا مِنْهُ"
items.translit: Whomever Allah has given food, should say:
items.en: Whomever Allah has given food, should say: O Allah, bless us in it and provide us with better than it. Whomever Allah has given milk to drink, should say: O Allah, bless us in it and give us more of it.

items.title: Praise for the food and the strength to receive it
items.arabic: الْحَمْـدُ للهِ الَّذي أَطْعَمَنـي هـذا وَرَزَقَنـيهِ مِنْ غَـيْرِ حَوْلٍ مِنِّي وَلا قُوَّة
items.translit: Alhamdu lillāhi ‘l-ladhī aṭ`amanī hādhā, wa razaqanīhi min ghayri ḥawlin minnī wa lā quwwah.
items.en: Praise is to Allah Who has given me this food, and sustained me with it though I was unable to do it and powerless.
items.note: To thank Allah not only for the provision but for the ability to take it, admitting you were powerless to provide it yourself. A reported reward is that past sins are forgiven.

items.title: Abundant, good and blessed praise
items.arabic: الْحَمْـدُ للهِ حَمْـداً كَثـيراً طَيِّـباً مُبـارَكاً فيه، غَيْرَ مَكْفِيٍّ وَلا مُوَدَّعٍ وَلا مُسْتَغْـنىً عَنْـهُ رَبَّـنا
items.translit: Alhamdu lillāhi ḥamdan kathīran tayyiban mubārakan fīh, ghayra makfiyyin wa lā muwadda`in, wa lā mustaghnan `anhu Rabbanā.
items.en: All praise is to Allah, praise in abundance, good and blessed. It cannot [be compensated for, nor can it] be left, nor can it be done without, our Lord.

items.title: A du'a for those who provided the food
items.arabic: اللّهُـمَّ بارِكْ لَهُمْ فيما رَزَقْـتَهُم، وَاغْفِـرْ لَهُـمْ وَارْحَمْهُمْ
items.translit: Allāhumma bārik lahum fī mā razaqtahum, wagh’fir lahum warḥamhum.
items.en: O Allah, bless them in what You have provided for them, and forgive them and have mercy on them.

items.title: For whoever fed you or gave you drink
items.arabic: اللّهُـمَّ أَطْعِمْ مَن أَطْعَمَني، وَاسْقِ مَن سقاني
items.translit: Allāhumma aṭ`im man aṭ`amanī wasqi man saqānī.
items.en: O Allah, feed the one who has fed me, and give drink to the one who has given me drink.

items.title: The fasting guest's du'a for the host
items.arabic: أَفْطَـرَ عِنْدَكُم الصّـائِمونَ وَأَكَلَ طَعامَـكُمُ الأبْـرار، وَصَلَّـتْ عَلَـيْكُمُ الملائِكَـة
items.translit: Afṭara `indakumuṣ-ṣā'imūn, wa akala ṭa`āmakumu ‘l-'abrār, wa ṣallat `alaykumu ‘l-malā'ikah.
items.en: With you, those who are fasting have broken their fast, you have fed those who are righteous, and the angels recite their prayers upon you.
items.ref: Abu Dawud 3/367, Ibn Majah 1/556, An-Nasa'i, 'Amalul-Yawm wal-Laylah 296-8
items.note: When a fasting person eats at someone's table, this is the du'a left for the host: that the fasting have broken their fast with them, the righteous have eaten their food, and the angels have prayed for them.

items.title: Answering an invitation to eat
items.arabic: وَمَعْنَى فَلْيُصَلِّ أَيْ فَلْيَدْعُ
items.en: When you are invited (to eat) then reply to the invitation. If you are fasting then invoke Allah's blessings (on your host), and if you are not fasting then eat.
items.note: The Prophet ﷺ taught that when you are invited you should respond: if you are fasting, pray for your host, and if not, then eat.

waking-up.name: Waking Up
waking-up.epithet: The first words of the day
waking-up.title: Waking Up: the adhkar to say the moment you open your eyes, in Arabic, transliteration and meaning
waking-up.meta_description: What to say when you wake up (adhkar al-istiyqadh): the praise, the dhikr of the night, and the verses the Prophet ﷺ recited on waking. Arabic, transliteration, meaning, source.
waking-up.keywords: waking up dua, adhkar when waking, dua after sleep, alhamdulillah alladhi ahyana, last ten verses of Aal-Imran, Hisn al-Muslim, Buruja
lede: Before your feet touch the floor, before the phone, there is a breath in which you were just returned to life. Sleep, the Qur'an says, is a lesser death, and waking is a small resurrection. The Prophet ﷺ met it with words.
These are the first words of the day: gratitude that you were given back your soul, and the verses he would recite as his eyes opened.
intro: Say these as you wake, before the day begins. They are short. The point is to make your very first thought a turning toward the One who held your soul through the night and chose to return it.
Then flow straight into the morning adhkar once you are up. Waking, then morning: the day opens in remembrance.
closing: You did nothing to earn the morning. Your heartbeat, your breath, the light, all of it was returned to you while you slept helpless. To wake with His name on your tongue is to start the day honest about who is holding it.
O Allah, You gave us back our souls this morning when You could have kept them. Make us grateful for the day, and make our first thought always of You.

faq.q: What is the first thing to say when you wake up?
faq.a: The Prophet ﷺ taught the words: Alhamdulillahil-ladhi ahyana ba'da ma amatana wa ilayhin-nushur, 'Praise is to Allah who gave us life after He caused us to die, and to Him is the return.' It is the first dhikr of waking.

faq.q: What did the Prophet ﷺ recite when he woke?
faq.a: Among the things he did was to recite the last ten verses of Surah Aal-Imran (3:190-200), the verses on the signs in the heavens and the earth. They are included here with audio.

faq.q: What if I wake in the middle of the night?
faq.a: There is a beautiful dhikr for that: declaring Allah's oneness and praising Him. The Prophet ﷺ said whoever says it and then asks Allah will be answered, and whoever then rises to pray, his prayer is accepted.

email.subject: The first words of the day
email.alt_subject: What to say the moment you wake
email.preview: Before the phone, before your feet hit the floor: the adhkar of waking.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Sleep, the Qur'an calls a lesser death; and waking, a small resurrection. You did nothing to earn the morning. Your breath and your soul were returned to you while you slept helpless, and the Prophet ﷺ met that moment with words rather than a scroll through the phone.

The first is simple: 'Praise is to Allah who gave us life after He caused us to die, and to Him is the return.' He would also recite the last ten verses of Surah Aal-Imran as his eyes opened. We have gathered the adhkar of waking here, each with Arabic, transliteration, meaning and recitation, so your very first thought can be a turning toward the One who held you through the night.

The full set is here:

social.hook:
Sleep is a lesser death. Waking is a small resurrection.

You did nothing to earn the morning.
social.caption:
The adhkar of waking up.

Before the phone, before your feet touch the floor: the Prophet ﷺ met the morning with gratitude that his soul had been returned, and with the last ten verses of Surah Aal-Imran.

Make your first thought a turning toward the One who held you through the night.

Full set with Arabic, transliteration, meaning and audio at buruja.com/adhkar/waking-up

#WakingUp #MorningRoutine #Adhkar #Dhikr #SunnahOfTheProphet #IslamicReminders #Buruja #Islam

The prophetic adhkar, their transliterations, references and gradings are transcribed verbatim from Hisn al-Muslim (Fortress of the Muslim) by Sa'eed al-Qahtani, via sunnah.com; al-Bukhari and Muslim taken as sahih, jayyid/reliable noted as hasan. Recitation audio: Qur'anic items by Mishary Rashid Alafasy (everyayah.com); prophetic adhkar from the official Hisn al-Muslim audio (hisnmuslim.com), aligned by dua number. Selection and ordering follow Hisn al-Muslim, with thanks to lifewithallah.com. Reflections are Buruja's own. FOR SCHOLAR REVIEW: confirm every Arabic text, reference, repetition count and grading before publication.

items.title: Praise for being given life again
items.arabic: الحَمْـدُ لِلّهِ الّذي أَحْـيانا بَعْـدَ ما أَماتَـنا وَإليه النُّـشور
items.translit: Alḥamdu lillāhil-ladhī 'aḥyānā ba`da mā 'amātanā wa 'ilayhin-nushūr.
items.en: Praise is to Allah Who gives us life after He has caused us to die and to Him is the return.

items.title: If you wake in the night
items.arabic: لا إلهَ إلاّ اللّهُ وَحْـدَهُ لا شَـريكَ له، لهُ المُلـكُ ولهُ الحَمـد، وهوَ على كلّ شيءٍ قدير، سُـبْحانَ اللهِ، والحمْـدُ لله، ولا إلهَ إلاّ اللهُ واللهُ أكبَر، وَلا حَولَ وَلا قوّة إلاّ باللّهِ العليّ العظيم، رَبِّ اغْفرْ لي
items.translit: Lā 'ilāha 'illallāhu waḥdahu la sharīka lahu, lahul-mulku wa lahul-ḥamdu, wa huwa 'alā kulli shay'in qadīr Subḥānallāhi, walḥamdu lillāhi, wa lā 'ilāha 'illallāhu, wallāhu 'akbar, wa lā ḥaula wa lā quwwata 'illā billāhil-`aliyyil-`aẓīm, rabbighfir lī.
items.en: There is none worthy of worship but Allah alone, Who has no partner, His is the dominion and to Him belongs all praise, and He is able to do all things. Glory is to Allah. Praise is to Allah. There is none worthy of worship but Allah. Allah is the Most Great. There is no might and no power except by Allah's leave, the Exalted, the Mighty. My Lord, forgive me.
items.note: If you stir in the night and say this, then ask Allah for something, the Prophet ﷺ taught that your du'a is answered; and if you rise to pray, your prayer is accepted.

items.title: When your soul is returned to you
items.arabic: الحمدُ للهِ الذي عافاني في جَسَدي وَرَدّ عَليّ روحي وَأَذِنَ لي بِذِكْرِه
items.translit: Al-ḥamdu lillāhil-ladhī `āfānī fī jasadī, wa radda `alayya rūḥī, wa 'adhina lī bidhikrihi.
items.en: Praise is to Allah Who gave strength to my body and returned my soul to me and permitted me to remember Him.

items.title: The last ten verses of Aal-Imran
items.arabic: إِنَّ فِى خَلْقِ ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱخْتِلَٰفِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ لَءَايَٰتٍ لِّأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَٰبِ
items.en: Indeed, in the creation of the heavens and the earth and the alternation of the night and the day are signs for those of understanding.
items.note: The Prophet ﷺ would recite these on waking. Recite from here to the end of the surah (3:190-200). Tap play to hear all ten.

evening-adhkar.name: Evening Adhkar
evening-adhkar.epithet: The remembrances to close your day
evening-adhkar.title: Evening Adhkar: the remembrances to close your day, in Arabic, transliteration and meaning
evening-adhkar.meta_description: The evening adhkar (adhkar al-masa): the same protective remembrances as the morning, recited from after Asr until sunset. Arabic, transliteration, meaning, source and count. Qur'an via quran.ai.
evening-adhkar.keywords: evening adhkar, adhkar al-masa, evening dhikr, evening remembrance, amsayna, what to say in the evening, Hisn al-Muslim, daily wird, Buruja
lede: As the day closes, the believer does again what he did at its start: hands the hours back to Allah, asks for shelter through the dark, and remembers the One who carried him through the day. The evening adhkar are the twin of the morning.
Recite them as the sun sinks. The same words that opened your day now close it: praise, protection, and the quiet trust that the One who held you by day holds you by night.
evening-adhkar.intro_heading: How to pray the evening wird
intro: The time is from after the Asr prayer until sunset, with a little leeway into the early night. These are very nearly the same adhkar as the morning, recited at the other end of the day.

The Arabic below is shown in its morning form. Almost every dhikr is word-for-word identical evening and morning; in the few that change, where the morning says 'we have entered the morning' (asbahna) or 'this day,' in the evening you say 'we have entered the evening' (amsayna) and 'this night.' We have noted each dua that changes, and the exact evening wording is being finalised with our reviewers before launch.
As in the morning, you need not do all of it. Hold a few: Ayat al-Kursi, the three Quls, and Sayyid al-Istighfar, and let the rest grow.
evening-adhkar.closing_heading: Why we close the day this way
closing: The evening adhkar are how the believer puts the day to bed: nothing left unguarded, nothing taken for granted, the whole of it handed back to the One who owns it. To say them is to refuse to let a single day pass without remembering Him at both its ends.
O Allah, as You brought us safely to the evening, carry us through the night, and let us be among those whose hearts remember You at the close of every day.

faq.q: When should I read the evening adhkar?
faq.a: From after the Asr prayer until sunset is the recommended window, with some leeway into the early part of the night. The point is to close the day in remembrance as you opened it.

faq.q: Are the evening adhkar the same as the morning?
faq.a: Almost entirely. They are the same protective remembrances; the main difference is that where the morning says asbahna and Allahumma bika asbahna (we have entered the morning), in the evening you say amsayna (we have entered the evening), and 'this day' becomes 'this night.' A couple of adhkar are specified for only one of the two times.

faq.q: Do I have to read every one every evening?
faq.a: No. These are the gathered evening adhkar of the sunnah, not an obligation to complete. Begin with a few you can keep, for example Ayat al-Kursi, the three Quls, and Sayyid al-Istighfar, and build from there. The Prophet ﷺ loved the deed that is small and constant.

email.subject: Close the day the way you opened it
email.alt_subject: The evening wird
email.preview: The evening adhkar: the same protection as the morning, recited as the sun sinks.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

As the day closes, the believer does again what he did at its start: he hands the hours back to Allah, asks for shelter through the dark, and remembers the One who carried him through the day.

The evening adhkar are the twin of the morning, recited from after Asr until sunset. Almost every word is the same; where the morning says 'we have entered the morning,' in the evening you say 'we have entered the evening.' Ayat al-Kursi for a guardian, the three Quls for protection, Sayyid al-Istighfar, the praise that hands the day back to Allah.

The full evening wird, with Arabic, transliteration, meaning, source and count, is here:

social.hook:
You opened the day by handing it to Allah.

Close it the same way.
social.caption:
The evening adhkar.

The twin of the morning, recited from after Asr until sunset: Ayat al-Kursi for a guardian through the night, the three Quls for protection, Sayyid al-Istighfar, and the praise that hands the day back to the One who owns it.

Where the morning says 'we have entered the morning,' the evening says 'we have entered the evening.' Same trust, both ends of the day.

Full wird with Arabic, transliteration, meaning and audio at buruja.com/adhkar/evening-adhkar

#EveningAdhkar #AdhkarAlMasa #Dhikr #Dua #HisnAlMuslim #IslamicReminders #Buruja #Islam

evening-adhkar.source_note: The evening adhkar are the same chapter as the morning in Hisn al-Muslim (duas 75-98). The Arabic shown is the GROUNDED morning form (Qur'anic items via quran.ai in Uthmani script + Saheeh International; prophetic adhkar verbatim from sunnah.com). Where the wording changes for the evening (asbahna -> amsayna, 'this day' -> 'this night'), the change is explained in plain English on the affected duas rather than rendered in unverified Arabic; the exact evening Arabic is held FOR SCHOLAR REVIEW before launch. Recitation audio is the official Hisn al-Muslim morning/evening audio (hisnmuslim.com) and Mishary Alafasy (everyayah.com) for the Qur'an. With thanks to lifewithallah.com; reflections are Buruja's own.

items.title: Ayat al-Kursi
items.arabic: ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ ٱلْقَيُّومُ ۚ لَا تَأْخُذُهُۥ سِنَةٌ وَلَا نَوْمٌ ۚ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ مَن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يَشْفَعُ عِندَهُۥٓ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۚ يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ ۖ وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَىْءٍ مِّنْ عِلْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّا بِمَا شَآءَ ۚ وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ ٱلسَّمَٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ ۖ وَلَا يَـُٔودُهُۥ حِفْظُهُمَا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْعَظِيمُ
items.en: Allah - there is no deity except Him, the Ever-Living, the Self-Sustaining. Neither drowsiness overtakes Him nor sleep. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission? He knows what is before them and what will be after them, and they encompass not a thing of His knowledge except for what He wills. His Kursi extends over the heavens and the earth, and their preservation tires Him not. And He is the Most High, the Most Great.
items.note: The greatest verse in the Qur'an. Whoever recites it in the evening is guarded until morning.

items.title: The three Quls
items.arabic:
قُلْ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ أَحَدٌ ۝ ٱللَّهُ ٱلصَّمَدُ ۝ لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ ۝ وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ كُفُوًا أَحَدٌۢ
قُلْ أَعُوذُ بِرَبِّ ٱلْفَلَقِ ۝ مِن شَرِّ مَا خَلَقَ ۝ وَمِن شَرِّ غَاسِقٍ إِذَا وَقَبَ ۝ وَمِن شَرِّ ٱلنَّفَّٰثَٰتِ فِى ٱلْعُقَدِ ۝ وَمِن شَرِّ حَاسِدٍ إِذَا حَسَدَ
قُلْ أَعُوذُ بِرَبِّ ٱلنَّاسِ ۝ مَلِكِ ٱلنَّاسِ ۝ إِلَٰهِ ٱلنَّاسِ ۝ مِن شَرِّ ٱلْوَسْوَاسِ ٱلْخَنَّاسِ ۝ ٱلَّذِى يُوَسْوِسُ فِى صُدُورِ ٱلنَّاسِ ۝ مِنَ ٱلْجِنَّةِ وَٱلنَّاسِ
items.en: Say, "He is Allah, [who is] One, Allah, the Eternal Refuge. He neither begets nor is born, Nor is there to Him any equivalent." / Say, "I seek refuge in the Lord of daybreak From the evil of that which He created And from the evil of darkness when it settles And from the evil of the blowers in knots And from the evil of an envier when he envies." / Say, "I seek refuge in the Lord of mankind, The Sovereign of mankind, The God of mankind, From the evil of the retreating whisperer - Who whispers [evil] into the breasts of mankind - From among the jinn and mankind."
items.note: Al-Ikhlas, Al-Falaq and An-Nas, three times each. They suffice you against everything.

items.arabic: أَصْبَحْنَا وَأَصْبَحَ الْمُلْكُ لِلَّهِ وَالْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ، لاَ إِلَهَ إلاَّ اللَّهُ وَحْدَهُ لاَ شَرِيكَ لَهُ، لَهُ الْمُلْكُ وَلَهُ الْحَمْدُ وَهُوَ عَلَى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ، رَبِّ أَسْأَلُكَ خَيْرَ مَا فِي هَذَا الْيَوْمِ وَخَيرَ مَا بَعْدَهُ، وَأَعُوذُ بِكَ مِنْ شَرِّ مَا فِي هَذَا الْيَوْمِ وَشَرِّ مَا بَعْدَهُ، رَبِّ أَعُوذُ بِكَ مِنَ الْكَسَلِ وَسُوءِ الْكِبَرِ، رَبِّ أَعُوذُ بِكَ مِنْ عَذَابٍ فِي النَّارِ وَعَذَابٍ فِي القَـبْر
items.translit: Aṣbaḥnā wa aṣbaḥal-mulku lillāh, walḥamdu lillāh, lā ilāha illallāhu waḥdahu lā sharīka lah, lahul-mulku wa lahul-ḥamd, wa huwa `alā kulli shay'in Qadīr. Rabbi as'aluka khayra mā fī hādha ‘l-yawmi wa khayra mā ba`dahu wa a`ūdhu bika min sharri mā fī hātha ‘l-yawmi wa sharri mā ba`dahu, Rabbi a`ūdhu bika minal-kasali, wa sū'il-kibar, Rabbi a`ūdhu bika min `adhābin fin-nāri wa `adhābin fil-qabr.
items.en: We have entered a new day and with it all dominion is Allah's. Praise is to Allah. None has the right to be worshipped but Allah alone, Who has no partner. To Allah belongs the dominion, and to Him is the praise and He is Able to do all things. My Lord, I ask You for the goodness of this day and of the days that come after it, and I seek refuge in You from the evil of this day and of the days that come after it. My Lord, I seek refuge in You from laziness and helpless old age. My Lord, I seek refuge in You from the punishment of Hell-fire, and from the punishment of the grave.
items.title: The kingdom is Allah's
items.note: Evening form: say 'We have entered the evening, and the dominion has entered the evening, belonging to Allah,' and ask for the good of this night and refuge from the evil of this night.

items.arabic: اللّهُـمَّ بِكَ أَصْـبَحْنا وَبِكَ أَمْسَـينا وَبِكَ نَحْـيا وَبِكَ نَمـوتُ وَإِلَـيْكَ النِّـشور وإذا أمسي فليقل : اللهم بك أمسينا وبك أصبحنا وبك نحيا وبك نموت وإليك المصير
items.translit: Allāhumma bika aṣbaḥnā, wa bika amsaynā, wa bika naḥyā, wa bika namūt, wa ilaykan-nushūr. When you say this in the evening you should say:
items.en: O Allah, by You we enter the morning and by You we enter the evening, by You we live and by You we die, and to You is the Final Return. O Allah, You bring us the end of the day as You bring us its beginning, You bring us life and you bring us death, and to You is our fate.
items.title: By You we enter morning and evening
items.note: Evening form: '...and by You we die, and to You is the return (al-masir).'

items.arabic: اللّهـمَّ أَنْتَ رَبِّـي لا إلهَ إلاّ أَنْتَ خَلَقْتَنـي وَأَنا عَبْـدُك وَأَنا عَلـى عَهْـدِكَ وَوَعْـدِكَ ما اسْتَـطَعْـت أَعـوذُ بِكَ مِنْ شَـرِّ ما صَنَـعْت أَبـوءُ لَـكَ بِنِعْـمَتِـكَ عَلَـيَّ وَأَبـوءُ بِذَنْـبي فَاغْفـِرْ لي فَإِنَّـهُ لا يَغْـفِرُ الذُّنـوبَ إِلاّ أَنْتَ
items.translit: Allāhumma anta Rabbī lā ilāha illā ant, khalaqtanī wa anā `abduk, wa anā `alā `ahdika wa wa`dika mastaṭa`t, a`ūdhu bika min sharri mā ṣana`t, abū'u laka bi ni`matika `alay, wa abū'u bidhanbī faghfir lī fa'innahu lā yaghfirudh-dhunūba illā ant.
items.en: O Allah, You are my Lord, there is none worthy of worship but You. You created me and I am your slave. I keep Your covenant, and my pledge to You so far as I am able. I seek refuge in You from the evil of what I have done. I admit to Your blessings upon me, and I admit to my misdeeds. Forgive me, for there is none who may forgive sins but You.
items.title: Sayyid al-Istighfar
items.note: The chief way of seeking forgiveness: whoever says it by night with certainty and dies before morning is among the people of Paradise.

items.arabic: اللّهُـمَّ إِنِّـي أَصْبَـحْتُ أَُشْـهِدُك ، وَأُشْـهِدُ حَمَلَـةَ عَـرْشِـك ، وَمَلائِكَتِك ، وَجَمـيعَ خَلْـقِك ، أَنَّـكَ أَنْـتَ اللهُ لا إلهَ إلاّ أَنْـتَ وَحْـدَكَ لا شَريكَ لَـك ، وَأَنَّ ُ مُحَمّـداً عَبْـدُكَ وَرَسـولُـك
items.translit: Allāhumma innī aṣbaḥtu ush-hiduka wa ush-hidu ḥamalata `arshik, wa malā'ikataka wajamī`a khalqik, annaka antallāhu lā ilāha illā ant, waḥdaka lā sharīka lak, wa anna Muḥammadan `abduka wa rasūluk.
items.en: O Allah, I have entered a new morning and call upon You and upon the bearers of Your Throne, upon Your angels and all creation to bear witness that surely You are Allah, there is none worthy of worship but You alone, You have no partners, and that Muhammad is Your slave and Your Messenger. (Recite four times in Arabic.)
items.title: Bearing witness before Allah
items.note: Evening form: 'O Allah, I have entered the evening (amsaytu) bearing witness...'

items.arabic: اللّهُـمَّ ما أَصْبَـَحَ بي مِـنْ نِعْـمَةٍ أَو بِأَحَـدٍ مِـنْ خَلْـقِك ، فَمِـنْكَ وَحْـدَكَ لا شريكَ لَـك ، فَلَـكَ الْحَمْـدُ وَلَـكَ الشُّكْـر
items.translit: Allāhumma mā aṣbaha bī min ni`matin aw bi aḥadin min khalqik, fa minka waḥdaka lā sharīka lak, falaka ‘l-ḥamdu wa lakash-shukr.
items.en: O Allah, whatever blessing has been received by me or anyone of Your creation is from You alone, You have no partner. All praise is for you and thanks is to You.
items.ref: When you say this in the evening, you should say: Allāhumma mā 'amsā bī...: "O Allah, as I
items.title: Every blessing is from You

items.arabic: اللّهُـمَّ عافِـني في بَدَنـي، اللّهُـمَّ عافِـني في سَمْـعي، اللّهُـمَّ عافِـني في بَصَـري، لا إلهَ إلاّ اللّه أَنْـتَ. اللّهُـمَّ إِنّـي أَعـوذُبِكَ مِنَ الْكُـفر، وَالفَـقْر، وَأَعـوذُ بِكَ مِنْ عَذابِ القَـبْر، لا إلهَ إلاّ أَنْـتَ
items.translit: Allāhumma `āfinī fī badanī, Allāhumma `āfinī fī sam`ī, Allāhumma `āfinī fī baṣarī, lā ilāha illā ant. Allāhumma innī a`ūdhu bika mina ‘l-kufri, wa ‘l-faqr, wa a`ūdhu bika min `adhābi ‘l-qabr, lā ilāha illā ant.
items.en: O Allah, make me healthy in my body. O Allah, preserve for me my hearing. O Allah, preserve for me my sight. There is none worthy of worship but You. O Allah, I seek refuge in You from disbelief and poverty, and I seek refuge in You from the punishment of the grave. There is none worthy of worship but You (Recite three times in Arabic.)
items.title: Soundness in body, hearing and sight

items.arabic: حَسْبِـيَ اللّهُ لا إلهَ إلاّ هُوَ عَلَـيهِ تَوَكَّـلتُ وَهُوَ رَبُّ العَرْشِ العَظـيم
items.translit: Ḥasbiyallāhu lā ilāha illā huwa `alayhi tawakkalt, wa huwa Rabbu ‘l-`Arshi ‘l-'Aẓīm.
items.en: Allah is sufficient for me. There is none worthy of worship but Him. I have placed my trust in Him, He is Lord of the Majestic Throne. (Recite seven times in Arabic.)
items.title: Allah is sufficient for me
items.note: Seven times. Allah suffices the one who says it in all that worries him.

items.arabic: اللّهُـمَّ إِنِّـي أسْـأَلُـكَ العَـفْوَ وَالعـافِـيةَ في الدُّنْـيا وَالآخِـرَة ، اللّهُـمَّ إِنِّـي أسْـأَلُـكَ العَـفْوَ وَالعـافِـيةَ في ديني وَدُنْـيايَ وَأهْـلي وَمالـي ، اللّهُـمَّ اسْتُـرْ عـوْراتي وَآمِـنْ رَوْعاتـي ، اللّهُـمَّ احْفَظْـني مِن بَـينِ يَدَيَّ وَمِن خَلْفـي وَعَن يَمـيني وَعَن شِمـالي ، وَمِن فَوْقـي ، وَأَعـوذُ بِعَظَمَـتِكَ أَن أُغْـتالَ مِن تَحْتـي
items.translit: Allāhumma innī as'aluka 'l-`afwa wal-`āfiyah fid-dunyā wal-ākhirah, Allāhumma innī as'aluka 'l-`afwa wal-`āfiyah fī dīnī wa dunyāya, wa ahlī, wa mālī, Allāhummastur `awrātī, wa āmin raw`ātī, Allāhummaḥfaẓnī min bayni yadayya, wa min khalfī, wa `an yamīnī, wa `an shimālī, wa min fawqī, wa a`ūdhu bi`aẓamatika an 'ughtāla min taḥtī.
items.en: O Allah, I seek Your forgiveness and Your protection in this world and the next. O Allah, I seek Your forgiveness and Your protection in my religion, in my worldly affairs, in my family and in my wealth. O Allah, conceal my secrets and preserve me from anguish. O Allah, guard me from what is in front of me and behind me, from my left, and from my right, and from above me. I seek refuge in Your Greatness from being struck down from beneath me.
items.title: Forgiveness and shelter, here and hereafter

items.arabic: اللّهُـمَّ عالِـمَ الغَـيْبِ وَالشّـهادَةِ فاطِـرَ السّماواتِ وَالأرْضِ رَبَّ كـلِّ شَـيءٍ وَمَليـكَه ، أَشْهَـدُ أَنْ لا إِلـهَ إِلاّ أَنْت ، أَعـوذُ بِكَ مِن شَـرِّ نَفْسـي وَمِن شَـرِّ الشَّيْـطانِ وَشِـرْكِه ، وَأَنْ أَقْتَـرِفَ عَلـى نَفْسـي سوءاً أَوْ أَجُـرَّهُ إِلـى مُسْـلِم
items.translit: Allāhumma `ālima ‘l-ghaybi wash-shahādah fātir as-samāwāti wa ‘l'arḍ, Rabba kulli shay'in wa malīkah, ash-hadu an lā ilāha illā ant, a`ūdhu bika min sharri nafsī, wa min sharrish-shayṭāni wa shirkih, wa an aqtarifa `alā nafsī sū'an, aw ajurrahu ilā Muslim.
items.en: O Allah, Knower of the unseen and the evident, Maker of the heavens and the earth, Lord of everything and its Possessor, I bear witness that there is none worthy of worship but You. I seek refuge in You from the evil of my soul, and from the evil of Satan and his helpers. (I seek refuge in You) from bringing evil upon my soul and from harming any Muslim.
items.title: Refuge with the Knower of the unseen

items.arabic: بِسـمِ اللهِ الذي لا يَضُـرُّ مَعَ اسمِـهِ شَيءٌ في الأرْضِ وَلا في السّمـاءِ وَهـوَ السّمـيعُ العَلـيم
items.translit: Bismillāhi ‘l-ladhī lā yaḍurru ma`a-smihi shay'un fil-'arḍi wa lā fis-samā' wa huwas-Samī `ul-`Alīm.
items.en: In the Name of Allah, Who with His Name nothing can cause harm in the earth nor in the heavens, and He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing. (Recite three times in Arabic).
items.ref: Abu Dawud 4/323, At-Tirmidhi 5/465, Ibn Majah 2/332, Ahmad
items.title: The Name with which nothing can harm
items.note: Three times. Nothing will harm him on earth or in the sky until morning.

items.arabic: رَضيـتُ بِاللهِ رَبَّـاً وَبِالإسْلامِ ديـناً وَبِمُحَـمَّدٍ نَبِيّـاً
items.translit: Raḍītu billāhi Rabba, wa bil-Islāmi dīna, wa bi-Muḥammadin (ṣallallāhu `alayhi wa sallama) nabiyya.
items.en: I am pleased with Allah as my Lord, with Islam as my religion, and with Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) as my Prophet. (Recite three times in Arabic.)
items.title: Pleased with Allah, Islam, and His Messenger ﷺ

items.arabic: يا حَـيُّ يا قَيّـومُ بِـرَحْمَـتِكِ أَسْتَـغـيث ، أَصْلِـحْ لي شَـأْنـي كُلَّـه ، وَلا تَكِلـني إِلى نَفْـسي طَـرْفَةَ عَـين
items.translit: Yā Ḥayyu yā Qayyūmu biraḥmatika astaghīth aṣlih lī sha'nī kullah wa lā takilnī ilā nafsī ṭarfata `ayn.
items.en: O Ever-Living One, O Eternal One, by Your mercy I call on You to set right all my affairs. Do not place me in charge of my soul even for the blinking of an eye (i.e. a moment).
items.title: O Ever-Living, O Sustainer

items.arabic: أَصْبَـحْـنا وَأَصْبَـحْ المُـلكُ للهِ رَبِّ العـالَمـين ، اللّهُـمَّ إِنِّـي أسْـأَلُـكَ خَـيْرَ هـذا الـيَوْم ، فَـتْحَهُ ، وَنَصْـرَهُ ، وَنـورَهُ وَبَـرَكَتَـهُ ، وَهُـداهُ ، وَأَعـوذُ بِـكَ مِـنْ شَـرِّ ما فـيهِ وَشَـرِّ ما بَعْـدَه وإذا أمسى قال: أَمْسَيْـنا وَأَمْسـى المُـلكُ للهِ رَبِّ العـالَمـين ، اللّهُـمَّ إِنِّـي أسْـأَلُـكَ خَـيْرَ هـذهِ اللَّـيْلَة ، فَتْحَهـا ، وَنَصْـرَهـا ، وَنـورَهـا وَبَـرَكَتَـهـا ، وَهُـداهـا ، وَأَعـوذُ بِـكَ مِـنْ شَـرِّ ما فـيهـاِ وَشَـرِّ ما بَعْـدَهـا
items.translit: Aṣbaḥnā wa aṣbaḥal-mulku lillāhi Rabbi ‘l-a`lāmīn, Allāhumma innī as'aluka khayra hādha ‘l-yawm:
items.en: We have entered a new day and with it all the dominion which belongs to Allah, Lord of all that exists. O Allah, I ask You for the goodness of this day,2 its victory, its help, its light, its blessings, and its guidance. I seek refuge in You from the evil that is in it, and from the evil that follows it.
items.title: Upon the dominion of Allah, Lord of all
items.note: Evening form: 'We have entered the evening upon the dominion of Allah... I ask You for the good of this night.'

items.arabic: أَصْـبَحْنا[أَمْسَـينا] علـى فِطْـرَةِ الإسْلام، وَعَلـى كَلِـمَةِ الإخْـلاص، وَعلـى دينِ نَبِـيِّنا مُحَـمَّدٍ وَعَلـى مِلَّـةِ أبينـا إِبْـراهيـمَ حَنيـفاً مُسْلِـماً وَمـا كـانَ مِنَ المُشـرِكيـن
items.translit: Aṣbahnā `alā fiṭrati ‘l-Islām, wa `alā kalimati ‘l-ikhlās, wa `alā dīni nabiyyinā Muḥammadin (ṣallallāhu `alayhi wa sallam), wa `alā millati abīnā Ibrāhīm, ḥanīfan Musliman wa mā kāna mina ‘l-mushrikīn.
items.en: We have entered a new day upon the natural religion of Islam, the word of sincere devotion, the religion of our Prophet Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), and the faith of our father Ibrahim. He was upright (in worshipping Allah), and a Muslim. He was not of those who worship others besides Allah.
items.title: Upon the fitrah of Islam
items.note: Evening form: 'We have entered the evening (amsayna) upon the fitrah of Islam...'

items.arabic: سُبْحـانَ اللهِ وَبِحَمْـدِهِ
items.translit: Subḥānallāhi wa biḥamdih.
items.en: Glory is to Allah and praise is to Him. (Recite one hundred times in Arabic).
items.title: Glory and praise to Allah
items.note: A hundred times. None comes on the Day of Judgement with better, save one who said the same or more.

items.arabic: لا إلهَ إلاّ اللّهُ وحْـدَهُ لا شَـريكَ لهُ، لهُ المُـلْكُ ولهُ الحَمْـد، وهُوَ على كُلّ شَيءٍ قَدير
items.translit: Lā ilāha illallāhu waḥdahu lā sharīka lah, lahu ‘l-mulku walahu ‘l-ḥamd, wa huwa `alā kulli shay'in qadīr.
items.en: None has the right to be worshipped but Allah alone, Who has no partner. His is the dominion and His is the praise and He is Able to do all things. (Recite ten times in Arabic or one time to ward off laziness.)
items.title: There is no god but Allah, alone

items.arabic: لا إلهَ إلاّ اللّهُ وحْـدَهُ لا شَـريكَ لهُ، لهُ المُـلْكُ ولهُ الحَمْـد، وهُوَ على كُلّ شَيءٍ قَدير
items.translit: Lā ilāha illallāhu waḥdahu lā sharīka lah, lahu ‘l-mulku walahu ‘l-ḥamd, wa huwa `alā kulli shay'in qadīr.
items.en: None has the right to be worshipped but Allah alone, Who has no partner. His is the dominion and His is the praise and He is Able to do all things. (Recite 100 times in Arabic upon rising in the morning).
items.title: There is no god but Allah, a hundredfold

items.arabic: سُبْحـانَ اللهِ وَبِحَمْـدِهِ عَدَدَ خَلْـقِه ، وَرِضـا نَفْسِـه ، وَزِنَـةَ عَـرْشِـه ، وَمِـدادَ كَلِمـاتِـه
items.translit: Subḥānallāhi wa biḥamdih:
items.en: Glory is to Allah and praise is to Him, by the multitude of His creation, by His Pleasure, by the weight of His Throne, and by the extent of His Words. (Recite three times in Arabic upon rising in the morning.)
items.title: Glory to Allah, by the number of His creation

items.arabic: اللَّهُمَّ إِنِّي أَسْأَلُكَ عِلْماً نَافِعاً، وَرِزْقاً طَيِّباً، وَعَمَلاً مُتَقَبَّلاً
items.translit: Allāhumma innī as'aluka `ilman nāfi`a, wa rizqan ṭayyiba, wa `amalan mutaqabbala.
items.en: O Allah, I ask You for knowledge that is of benefit, a good provision, and deeds that will be accepted. (Recite in Arabic upon rising in the morning.)
items.title: Asking for beneficial knowledge
items.note: Note: the Prophet ﷺ specified this du'a for the morning only; it is not part of the evening adhkar.

items.arabic: أَسْتَغْفِرُ اللَّهَ وَأَتُوبُ إِلَيْهِ
items.translit: Astaghfirullāha wa atūbu ilayh.
items.en: I seek the forgiveness of Allah and repent to Him. (Recite one hundred times in Arabic during the day.)
items.title: I seek Allah's forgiveness and turn to Him

items.arabic: أَعـوذُ بِكَلِمـاتِ اللّهِ التّـامّـاتِ مِنْ شَـرِّ ما خَلَـق
items.translit: A`ūdhu bikalimāti-llāhit-tāmmāti min sharri mā khalaq.
items.en: I seek refuge in the Perfect Words of Allah from the evil of what He has created. (Recite three times in Arabic in the evening.)
items.title: Refuge in the perfect words of Allah
items.note: Three times in the evening especially: whoever says it will not be harmed by anything that night.

items.arabic: اللَّهُمَّ صَلِّ وَ سَلِّمْ عَلَى نَبِيِّنَا مُحَمَّدٍ
items.translit: Allāhumma ṣalli wa sallim `alā nabiyyinā Muḥammad.
items.en: O Allah, we ask for your peace and blessings upon our Prophet Muhammad. (Recite ten times in Arabic.)
items.ref: The Prophet (ﷺ) said: "Who recites blessings upon me ten times in the morning and ten times in the evening will obtain my intercession on the Day of Resurrection
items.title: Blessings upon the Prophet ﷺ
items.note: Ten times in the morning and ten in the evening, to be reached by his intercession.

travel.name: Travel
travel.title: Travel Du'as: the supplications for setting out, the journey and returning, in Arabic, transliteration and meaning
travel.meta_description: The sunnah du'as of travel: mounting your transport, setting out, entering a town, bidding farewell, and returning home. Arabic, transliteration, meaning and source, verified.
travel.keywords: travel dua, dua for journey, dua when boarding transport, dua entering a town, dua for the traveller, dua returning from journey, dua al-safar, Hisn al-Muslim, Buruja
lede: A journey is a stretch of road you cannot see the end of, in the care of hands that are not your own. The Prophet ﷺ did not set out without acknowledging that. As he took his seat to travel he would glorify the One who tamed the journey for him, recite the verse, 'Glory to Him who subjected this to us, and we could not have subdued it ourselves, and to our Lord we will surely return,' and ask Allah for ease, company and a safe return.
These are the words of the road: for mounting your transport, setting out, arriving somewhere new, parting from those you love, and coming home.
intro: Say the riding du'a as you settle into your seat, whatever carries you, and the travel du'a as you set out. There are words for entering a town, for the rise and fall of the road, and for the moment you stop to rest. When you part from someone, leave them in Allah's keeping, and say the words of return when you are on your way home.

You do not need all of them for a short trip. The riding du'a and the prayer for the journey are the heart of it. Recite the Arabic, lean on the transliteration while you learn, and let the meaning travel with you.
closing: Travel strips away the illusion that you are in charge. You do not hold the road, the weather, the engine, or the hour of your return; He does. To set out with these words is to admit it gladly, to hand the journey to the only One who can see its end, and to remember that every road, in the end, is a road back to Him.
O Allah, be our Companion on the road and the Guardian of those we leave behind. Lighten our journey, fold up its distance, and bring us home in safety and faith.

faq.q: What do you say when you start a journey?
faq.a: As you take your seat on whatever carries you, say the riding du'a: Bismillah, then the praise, then the verse 'Glory to Him who subjected this to us...' (Surah az-Zukhruf 43:13-14). The Prophet ﷺ would follow it with the fuller travel du'a, asking Allah for goodness and piety on the journey, for ease, and for protection.

faq.q: Why does the travel du'a quote the Qur'an?
faq.a: The riding and travel du'as both recite Surah az-Zukhruf 43:13-14, the verse on Allah subjecting the means of travel to us. It is a reminder, said at the very moment you depend on that transport, that you could never have tamed it yourself, and that the journey ends with returning to your Lord.

faq.q: What do you say when leaving someone or being left behind?
faq.a: The one traveling says to those who stay, 'I place you in the trust of Allah, whose trust is never misplaced.' Those staying behind bid the traveller, 'I leave your religion in the care of Allah, as well as your safety, and the last of your deeds,' and ask Allah to give them piety as provision and make goodness easy for them.

faq.q: What do you say when you come back from a journey?
faq.a: On returning, the Prophet ﷺ would say Allahu Akbar three times from every elevated point, then declare Allah's oneness and add: 'We return repentant to our Lord, worshipping our Lord, and praising our Lord. He fulfilled His promise, He aided His slave, and He alone defeated the Confederates.'

email.subject: Words for the road
email.alt_subject: What the Prophet ﷺ said when he set out
email.preview: The du'as of travel: setting out, the journey, parting, and coming home.
email.body: Assalamu alaikum, A journey is a stretch of road you cannot see the end of, in the care of hands that are not your own. The Prophet ﷺ did not set out without acknowledging that. As he took his seat he would glorify the One who tamed the journey for him, recite 'Glory to Him who subjected this to us, and we could not have subdued it ourselves, and to our Lord we will surely return,' and ask Allah for ease, company and a safe return. We have gathered the du'as of travel, each with Arabic, a transliteration to help you learn it, the meaning and its source: for mounting your transport, setting out, entering a town, parting from those you love, and coming home. You do not need all of them for a short trip; the riding du'a and the prayer for the journey are the heart of it. Travel strips away the illusion that you are in charge. You do not hold the road, the weather, or the hour of your return; He does. To set out with these words is to hand the journey to the only One who can see its end, and to remember that every road, in the end, is a road back to Him. The full set, formatted to read or recite, is here:

social.hook: A journey is a road you cannot see the end of, in hands that are not your own. The Prophet ﷺ never set out without a word.
social.caption: The du'as of travel. For the moment you take your seat, for setting out, for entering a new place, for parting from those you love, and for coming home. The riding du'a recites the verse: 'Glory to Him who subjected this to us, and we could not have subdued it ourselves.' Travel strips away the illusion that you are in charge. To set out with these words is to hand the journey to the One who can see its end. Save this, and read the full set with Arabic, transliteration and meaning at buruja.com/adhkar/travel #TravelDua #DuaForJourney #DuaAlSafar #SunnahOfTheProphet #Dhikr #Dua #IslamicReminders #Buruja #Islam

travel.source_note: Prophetic adhkar transcribed verbatim from Hisn al-Muslim (Sa'eed al-Qahtani) via sunnah.com; al-Bukhari and Muslim taken as sahih, jayyid/reliable as hasan. Recitation audio: the official Hisn al-Muslim audio (hisnmuslim.com) for the adhkar. Selection follows Hisn al-Muslim, with thanks to lifewithallah.com; framing is Buruja's own. FOR SCHOLAR REVIEW: confirm every Arabic text, reference, repetition count and grading before publication.

items.title: The du'a for mounting your transport
items.arabic: بِسْـمِ اللهِ وَالْحَمْـدُ لله، سُبْحَانَ الَّذِي سَخَّرَ لَنَا هَذَا وَمَا كُنَّا لَهُ مُقْرِنِينَ * وَإِنَّا إِلَى رَبِّنَا لَمُنقَلِبُونَ الحَمْـدُ لله، الحَمْـدُ لله، الحَمْـدُ لله، اللهُ أكْـبَر، اللهُ أكْـبَر، اللهُ أكْـبَر، سُـبْحانَكَ اللّهُـمَّ إِنّي ظَلَـمْتُ نَفْسي فَاغْـفِرْ لي، فَإِنَّهُ لا يَغْفِـرُ الذُّنوبَ إِلاّ أَنْـت
items.translit: Bismillāh, walḥamdulillāh. Subḥāna ‘lladhī sakhkhara lanā hādhā wa mā kunnā lahu muqrinīn. Wa innā ilā Rabbinā lamunqalibūn. Alḥamdulillāh, alḥamdulillāh, alḥamdulillāh, Allāhu Akbar, Allāhu Akbar, Allāhu Akbar, Subḥānaka ‘llāhumma innī ẓalamtu nafsī, faghfir lī, fa innahu lā yaghfirudh-dhunūba illā ant.
items.en: With the Name of Allah. Praise is to Allah. Glory is to Him Who has provided this for us though we could never have had it by our efforts. Surely, unto our Lord, we are returning. Praise is to Allah. Praise is to Allah. Praise is to Allah. Allah is the Most Great. Allah is the Most Great. Allah is the Most Great. Glory is to You. O Allah, I have wronged my own soul. Forgive me, for surely none forgives sins but You.
items.note: Said as you settle into whatever carries you. It recites the verse of Surah az-Zukhruf, 'Glory to Him who subjected this to us, and we could not have subdued it ourselves,' then praises Allah, declares His greatness, and asks His forgiveness.

items.title: The full du'a for travel
items.arabic: "اللَّهُ أَكْبَرُ، اللَّهُ أَكْبَرُ، اللَّهُ أَكْبَرُ، سُبْحَانَ الَّذِي سَخَّرَ لَنَا هَذَا وَمَا كُنَّا لَهُ مُقْرِنِينَ * وَإِنَّا إِلَى رَبِّنَا لَمُنقَلِبُونَ اللَّهُمَّ إِنّا نَسْأَلُكَ فِي سَفَرِنَا هَذَا البِرَّ وَالتَّقْوَى، وَمِنَ الْعَمَلِ مَا تَرْضَى، اللَّهُمَّ هَوِّنْ عَلَيْنَا سَفَرَنَا هَذَا وَاطْوِ عَنَّا بُعْدَهُ، اللَّهُمَّ أَنْتَ الصَّاحِبُ فِي السَّفَرِ، وَالْخَليفَةُ فِي الْأَهْلِ، اللَّهُمَّ إِنِّي أَعُوذُ بِكَ مِنْ وَعْثَاءِ السَّفَرِ، وَكَآبَةِ الْمَنْظَرِ، وَسُوءِ الْمُنْقَلَبِ فِي الْمَالِ وَالْأَهْلِ"، وإذا رَجَعَ قَالَهُنَّ وَزَادَ فِيهِنَّ: "آيِبُونَ، تائِبُونَ، عَابِدُونَ، لِرَبِّنَا حَامِدُونَ"
items.translit: Allāhu Akbar, Allāhu Akbar, Allāhu Akbar, Subḥāna ‘l-ladhi sakhkhara lanā hādhā wa mā kunnā lahu muqrinīn. Wa innā ilā Rabbinā lamunqalibūn. Allāhumma innā nas'aluka fī safarinā hādha ‘l-birra wat-taqwā, Wa mina ‘l-`amali mā tarḍā, Allāhumma hawwin `alaynā safaranā hādhā waṭwi `annā bu`dah, Allāhumma antas-sāḥibu fis-safar, wa ‘l-khalīfatu fil-ahl, Allāhumma innī a`ūdhu bika min wa`thā'is-safar, wa ka'ābati ‘l-manẓar, wa sū'il-munqalabi fil-māli wa ‘l-ahl. (Upon returning recite the same again adding):
items.en: Allah is the Most Great. Allah is the Most Great. Allah is the Most Great. Glory is to Him Who has provided this for us though we could never have had it by our efforts. Surely, unto our Lord we are returning. O Allah, we ask You on this our journey for goodness and piety, and for works that are pleasing to You. O Allah, lighten this journey for us and make its distance easy for us. O Allah, You are our Companion on the road and the One in Whose care we leave our family. O Allah, I seek refuge in You from this journey's hardships, and from the wicked sights in store and from finding our family and property in misfortune upon returning. (Upon returning recite the same again adding:) We return repentant to our Lord, worshipping our Lord, and praising our Lord.
items.note: The fuller supplication of setting out: after the az-Zukhruf verse, you ask Allah for goodness, piety and pleasing deeds on the journey, for the distance to be made easy, for His company on the road and His care over your family, and for refuge from the journey's hardships. On returning you say it again, adding the words of the homecomer.

items.title: Entering a town or village
items.arabic: أللّـهُمَّ رَبَّ السَّـمواتِ السّـبْعِ وَما أَظْلَلَـن، وَرَبَّ الأَراضيـنَ السّـبْعِ وَما أقْلَلْـن، وَرَبَّ الشَّيـاطينِ وَما أَضْلَلْـن، وَرَبَّ الرِّياحِ وَما ذَرَيْـن، أَسْـأَلُـكَ خَيْـرَ هذهِ الْقَـرْيَةِ وَخَيْـرَ أَهْلِـها، وَخَيْـرَ ما فيها، وَأَعـوذُ بِكَ مِنْ شَـرِّها وَشَـرِّ أَهْلِـها، وَشَـرِّ ما فيها
items.translit: Allāhumma Rabbas-samāwātis-sab`i wa mā aẓlaln, Wa Rabba ‘l-arāḍīnas-sab`i wa mā aqlaln, wa Rabbash-shayāṭīni wa mā aḍlaln, wa Rabbar-riyāḥi wa mā dharayn. As'aluka khayra hādhihi ‘l-qaryah, wa khayra ahlihā, wa khayra māfīhā, wa a`ūdhu bika min sharrihā, wa sharri ahlihā, wa sharri mā fīhā.
items.en: O Allah, Lord of the seven heavens and all they overshadow, Lord of the seven worlds and all they uphold, Lord of the devils and all they lead astray, Lord of the winds and all they scatter. I ask You for the goodness of this town, and for the goodness of its people, and for the goodness it contains. I seek refuge in You from its evil, from the evil of its people, and from the evil it contains.

items.title: When your transport stumbles
items.arabic: بِسْـمِ اللهِ
items.translit: Bismillāh.
items.en: With the Name of Allah.
items.note: If your mount, or whatever carries you, stumbles or falters, the word is simply Bismillah, the Name of Allah, rather than cursing the moment.

items.title: The traveller's du'a for those left behind
items.arabic: أَسْتَـوْدِعُكُـمُ اللَّهَ الَّذي لا تَضـيعُ وَدائِعُـه
items.translit: Astawdi`ukumu ‘llāha ‘l-ladhi lā taḍī`u wadā'i`uh.
items.en: I place you in the trust of Allah, whose trust is never misplaced.
items.note: As you depart, you place those who stay in Allah's keeping: His trust, the Prophet ﷺ taught, is never lost or misplaced.

items.title: Bidding farewell to a traveller
items.arabic: أَسْتَـوْدِعُ اللَّهَ ديـنَكَ وَأَمانَتَـكَ، وَخَـواتيـمَ عَمَـلِك
items.translit: Astawdi`ullāha dīnak, wa amānatak, wa khawātīma `amalik.
items.en: I leave your religion in the care of Allah, as well as your safety, and the last of your deeds.
items.note: What those staying behind say to the one setting out, entrusting to Allah's care the three things that matter most: their religion, their safety, and the final deeds of their life.

items.title: A blessing for the one departing
items.arabic: زَوَّدَكَ اللَّهُ التقْوى، وَغَفَـرَذَنْـبَكَ، وَيَسَّـرَ لَكَ الخَـيْرَ حَيْـثُما كُنْـت
items.translit: Zawwadaka ‘llāhut-taqwā, wa ghafara dhanbak, wa yassara laka ‘l-khayra ḥaythu mā kunt.
items.en: May Allah give you piety as your provision, forgive your sins, and make goodness easy for you wherever you are.

items.title: Going up and coming down
items.arabic: قَالَ جَابِرٌ رضي الله عنه:
items.en: When we climbed we declared Allah's greatness (Allahu Akbar), and when we descended we glorified Him (Subhan Allah).
items.note: Jabir reported that as they climbed they would say Allahu Akbar, and as they descended they would say Subhanallah, marking the rise and fall of the road with remembrance.

items.title: A du'a on the journey at dawn
items.arabic: سَمِـعَ سـامِعٌ بِحَمْـدِ اللهِ وَحُسْـنِ بَلائِـهِ عَلَيْـنا. رَبَّنـا صـاحِبْـنا وَأَفْـضِل عَلَيْـنا عائِذاً باللهِ مِنَ النّـار
items.translit: Sami`a sāmi`un biḥamdillāhi wa ḥusni balā'ihi `alaynā. Rabbanā ṣāḥibnā, wa afḍil `alaynā, `ā'idhan billāhi minan-nār.
items.en: He Who listens has heard that we praise Allah for the good things He gives us. Our Lord, be with us and bestow Your favor upon us. I seek the protection of Allah from the Fire.
items.ref: Muslim 4/2086, the meaning of sami`a sāmi`un (who listens has heard) is that 'a witness has witnessed our praise of Allah due to His blessings and favor upon us

items.title: When you stop to rest
items.arabic: أَعـوذُ بِكَلِـماتِ اللّهِ التّـامّاتِ مِنْ شَـرِّ ما خَلَـق
items.translit: A`ūdhu bikalimāti ‘llāhit-tāmmāti min sharri mā khalaq.
items.en: I seek refuge in the Perfect Words of Allah from the evil of what He has created.
items.note: On alighting at any place along the way, seek refuge in the Perfect Words of Allah from the evil of what He has created. The Prophet ﷺ taught that whoever says it, nothing will harm him until he leaves that stop.

items.title: Returning home from a journey
items.arabic: يُكَبِّرُ عَلَى كُلِّ شَرَفٍ ثَلاَثَ تَكْبِيرَاتٍ ثُمَّ يَقُولُ: لاَ إِلَهَ إِلاَّ اللَّهُ وَحْدَهُ لاَ شَرِيكَ لَهُ، لَهُ الْمُلْكُ، وَلَهُ الْحَمْدُ، وَهُوَ عَلَى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ، آيِبُونَ، تَائِبُونَ، عَابِدُونَ، لِرَبِّنا حَامِدُونَ، صَدَقَ اللَّهُ وَعْدَهُ، وَنَصَرَ عَبْدَهُ، وَهَزَمَ الْأَحْزابَ وَحْدَهُ
items.translit: From every elevated point say Allāhu Akbar (three times), and then recite:
items.en: From every elevated point say Allāhu Akbar (Allah is the Most Great) three times and then recite: None has the right to be worshipped but Allah alone, Who has no partner. His is the dominion and His is the praise, and He is Able to do all things. We return repentant to our Lord, worshipping our Lord, and praising our Lord. He fulfilled His Promise, He aided His slave, and He alone defeated the Confederates.
items.note: The homecoming du'a: Allahu Akbar three times from every high point, then declaring Allah's oneness and adding the words of the returning believer, repentant, worshipping and praising, affirming that Allah kept His promise and aided His servant.

marriage-children.name: Marriage & Children
marriage-children.epithet: The du'as for the home you are building
marriage-children.title: Marriage & Children Du'as: the supplications for marriage and your children in Arabic, transliteration and meaning
marriage-children.meta_description: Du'as for the newly married, before intimacy, congratulating a newborn, and seeking Allah's protection for your children, in Arabic, transliteration and English with sources.
marriage-children.keywords: marriage dua, dua for newly married, dua before intimacy, dua for a newborn, congratulating a newborn in Islam, protection for children, dua for children, Hisn al-Muslim, Buruja
lede: A home does not begin with the keys or the furniture. It begins with a few words handed up to Allah: bless this, keep the shaytan out of it, let what grows here be good. The Prophet ﷺ gave the believers exactly those words, for the day two people are joined, for the night they come together, and for the children Allah trusts to their care.

These are the supplications of the family. Say them over a new marriage, before intimacy, over a newborn laid in your arms, and over the children you would protect with your life. You are asking the One who gave them to you to guard what you cannot.
intro: Match the du'a to the moment. There is the du'a you say to a couple just married, the husband's du'a over his wife at the start, and the words said quietly before intimacy. For a newborn there are the words of congratulation with their reply, and the Prophet's ﷺ own seeking of refuge for his grandchildren.

Recite the Arabic, lean on the transliteration while it is still new on your tongue, and read the meaning so your heart knows what you are asking for. None of these are long. Said with presence, each is the work of a breath.
closing: Marriage and children are among the heaviest trusts Allah gives, and the most beloved. The sunnah does not leave you to carry them alone. It hands you words for the joining, words for the bed, words over the cradle, and a shield of refuge for every child you raise. Say them, and keep saying them, and let the home you are building rest in hands that never tire.

O Allah, bless what You have joined and what You have given, keep the shaytan far from our spouses and our children, and let every soul under our roof be a coolness to our eyes and a people of Paradise.

faq.q: What do you say to congratulate someone on a new baby in Islam?
faq.a: The transmitted words ask Allah to bless the gift, to let the new parents give thanks, and to let the child reach maturity in righteousness. The parent then replies, asking Allah to bless and reward the one who congratulated them. It is a far richer exchange than a simple 'congratulations'.

faq.q: Is there a du'a to say before intimacy with my spouse?
faq.a: Yes. The Prophet ﷺ taught that if a husband says 'Bismillah, O Allah keep the shaytan away from us and from what You provide for us' before intimacy, then if a child is decreed from that union, the shaytan will never be able to harm it. Say it quietly between yourselves.

faq.q: How did the Prophet ﷺ seek protection for children?
faq.a: He would seek refuge for his grandsons al-Hasan and al-Husayn in the Perfect Words of Allah from every devil, every harmful creature, and every envious eye. It is a sunnah you can keep over your own children, by day or at night.

email.subject: The du'as for the home you are building
email.alt_subject: What the Prophet ﷺ said over a new marriage and a newborn
email.preview: Words for the joining, the night, and the children, in Arabic, transliteration and meaning.
email.body: Assalamu alaikum, A home does not begin with the keys or the furniture. It begins with a few words handed up to Allah: bless this, keep the shaytan out of it, let what grows here be good. The Prophet ﷺ gave the believers exactly those words, for the day two people are joined, for the night they come together, and for the children Allah trusts to their care. We have gathered the du'as of the family, each with its Arabic, a transliteration to help you learn it, the meaning, and its source. There is the du'a for a couple just married, the husband's du'a over his wife, the quiet supplication before intimacy, the words of congratulation over a newborn with their reply, and the very refuge the Prophet ﷺ sought for his own grandchildren. Marriage and children are among the heaviest trusts Allah gives, and the most beloved. The sunnah does not leave you to carry them alone. The full collection, formatted to read or recite, is here:

items.title: Congratulating new parents (and the reply)
items.arabic: بَارَكَ اللَّهُ لَكَ فِي الْمَوْهُوبِ لَكَ، وَشَكَرْتَ الْوَاهِبَ، وَبَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُ، وَرُزِقْتَ بِرَّهُ. وَيَرُدُّ عَلَيْهِ الْمُهَــــــنَّأُ فَيَقُولُ: بَارَكَ اللَّهُ لَكَ وَبَارَكَ عَلَيْكَ، وَجَزَاكَ اللَّهُ خَيْراً، وَرَزَقَكَ اللَّهُ مِثْلَهُ، وَأَجْزَلَ ثَوَابَكَ
items.translit: Bārakallāhu laka fi ‘l-mawhūbi lak, wa shakarta ‘l-wāhib, wa balagha ashuddah, wa ruziqta birrah. The reply of the person being congratulated is to say:
items.en: May Allah bless you with His gift to you, and may you (the new parent) give thanks, may the child reach the maturity of years, and may you be granted its righteousness. The reply of the person being congratulated is to say: May Allah bless you, and shower His blessings upon you, and may Allah reward you well and bestow upon you its like and reward you abundantly
items.note: The words to greet a new birth: a prayer that Allah bless the gift, that the parents give thanks, and that the child reach maturity in righteousness. The parent then replies, asking Allah to reward and bless the one who came to congratulate them.

items.title: Seeking refuge for a child
items.arabic: كان رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم يعوذ الحسن والحسين " أعيذكما بكلمات الله التامة ، من كل شيطان وهامة ، ومن كل عينِ لامة "
items.translit: The Prophet (ﷺ) used to seek Allah's protection for Al-Hasan and Al-Husain by saying:
items.en: I seek protection for you in the Perfect Words of Allah from every devil and every beast, and from every envious blameworthy eye.
items.note: The Prophet ﷺ would say this over his grandsons al-Hasan and al-Husayn, seeking their protection in the Perfect Words of Allah from every devil, every harmful creature, and every envious eye. A shield to keep over your own children.

items.title: For the newly married
items.arabic: بارَكَ اللّهُ لَك، وَبارَكَ عَلَـيْك، وَجَمَعَ بَيْـنَكُما في خَـيْر
items.translit: Bārakallāhu lak, wa bāraka `alayk, wa jama`a baynakumā fī khayr.
items.en: May Allah bless you, and shower His blessings upon you, and join you together in goodness.
items.note: What you say to a couple just married: that Allah bless each of them, pour His blessing over them, and join them together in goodness. The du'a to greet a new marriage with.

items.title: A husband's du'a over his wife
items.arabic: إِذَا تَزَوَّجَ أَحَدُكُمُ امْرَأَةً، أَوْ إِذَا اشْتَرَى خَادِماً فَلْيَقُلْ: اللَّهُمَّ إِنِّي أَسْأَلُكَ خَيْرَهَا، وَخَيْرَ مَا جَبَلْتَهَا عَلَيْهِ، وَأَعُوذُ بِكَ مِنْ شَرِّهَا، وَشَرِّ مَا جَبَلْتَهَا عَلَيْهِ، وَإِذَا اشْتَرَى بَعِيراً فَلْيَأْخُذْ بِذِرْوَةِ سَنَامِهِ وَلْيَقُلْ مِثْلَ ذَلِكَ
items.translit: When any of you marries a woman or purchases a maid-servant then let him say:
items.en: When any of you marries a woman or purchases a maid-servant then let him say: O Allah, I ask You for the goodness of her, and the goodness upon which You have created her, and I seek refuge in You from the evil of her, and from the evil upon which You have created her. If you purchase a camel then take hold of the top of its hump and say the same.
items.note: Taught for the start of a marriage: asking Allah for the goodness in her and the goodness she was created upon, and seeking refuge from any evil in her or in her nature. A du'a of trust at the very beginning.

items.title: Before intimacy
items.arabic: بِسْمِ الله اللّهُـمَّ جَنِّبْنا الشَّيْـطانَ، وَجَنِّبِ الشَّـيْطانَ ما رَزَقْـتَنا
items.translit: Bismillāh. Allāhumma jannibnash-Shayṭān, wa jannibish-Shayṭāna mā razaqtanā.
items.en: With the Name of Allah. O Allah, keep the Devil away from us, and keep the Devil away from that which You provide for us.
items.note: Said quietly between husband and wife. The Prophet ﷺ taught that if a child is decreed from a union begun with these words, the shaytan will never be able to harm it.

money-shopping.name: Money & Shopping
money-shopping.epithet: Keeping Allah in mind where the world is loudest
money-shopping.title: Money & Shopping Du'as: supplications for the marketplace and settling debt in Arabic, transliteration and meaning
money-shopping.meta_description: Du'as for entering the market, seeking sufficiency through the lawful, and relief from debt, in Arabic, transliteration and English, each with its source and grading.
money-shopping.keywords: dua for the market, dua entering the marketplace, dua for debt, dua to pay off debt, dua for sufficiency, halal provision dua, dua for rizq, Hisn al-Muslim, Buruja
lede: The marketplace is where the heart is most easily pulled away. It is loud, it is busy, and it asks you to forget. The Prophet ﷺ called it the most beloved place to the shaytan, and he gave the believer words to carry into it, words that turn a place of forgetfulness into a place of remembrance and reward.

These are the du'as for money and the market: a plea for Allah to make the lawful enough for you so you never need the forbidden, refuge from the weight of debt, and the remembrance to say when you step into the noise of buying and selling.
intro: Number 209 is the du'a of the marketplace, said as you enter; the Prophet ﷺ promised an immense reward and the wiping away of sins for the one who declares Allah's oneness there. Numbers 136 and 137 are for the weight money can put on the heart: one asks Allah to make His lawful provision enough for you, the other seeks refuge from worry, debt, and being overpowered.
Recite the Arabic, lean on the transliteration while you learn it, and read the meaning so your heart is present. Say them where you would normally forget, and the errand becomes worship.
closing: Rizq is from Allah alone, and so is contentment with it. These few words ask Him for both: that the lawful be enough, that the heart be free of the world's grip, and that the burden of debt be lifted before it crushes. Carry them into the market and over your accounts, and let your provision come from the only Hand that gives without ever running short.
O Allah, suffice us with Your lawful so we never reach for the forbidden, free us of all need but the need for You, and lift from us the weight of debt and the worry that travels with it.

faq.q: What is the du'a for entering the marketplace?
faq.a: It is the declaration of Allah's oneness in du'a 209, said as you enter. The Prophet ﷺ taught that whoever says it in the market has a million good deeds written for him, a million sins wiped away, and is raised a million ranks. The market is where people forget Allah, so remembering Him there carries an immense reward.

faq.q: Is there a du'a to help pay off debt?
faq.a: Yes. Du'a 137 seeks refuge from worry, grief, and the specific burden of debt and being overpowered by men. Alongside it, du'a 136 asks Allah to make His lawful provision enough for you and to free you of need for anyone besides Him, which is the root of being able to settle what you owe.

faq.q: Can asking for money really be an act of worship?
faq.a: When you ask Allah for lawful provision, name Him as your sole provider, and seek contentment with what He gives, the request itself is worship. These du'as turn money, the market, and even debt into reasons to turn back to Him rather than away.

The marketplace is where the heart is most easily pulled away. It is loud, it is busy, and it asks you to forget. The Prophet ﷺ called it the most beloved place to the shaytan, and he gave the believer words to carry into it, words that turn a place of forgetfulness into a place of remembrance and reward.

We have gathered the du'as for money and the market, each with its Arabic, a transliteration to help you learn it, the meaning, and its source. There is the du'a of the marketplace, with its immense reward for the one who remembers Allah where others forget Him; a plea for Allah to make His lawful enough for you so you never need the forbidden; and refuge from the weight of debt and the worry that travels with it.

Rizq is from Allah alone, and so is contentment with it. Carry these into the market and over your accounts.

items.title: Make the lawful enough for me
items.arabic: اللّهُـمَّ اكْفِـني بِحَلالِـكَ عَنْ حَـرامِـك، وَأَغْنِـني بِفَضْـلِكَ عَمَّـنْ سِـواك
items.translit: Allāhummak-finī biḥalālika `an ḥarāmik, wa 'aghninī bi faḍlika `amman siwāk.
items.en: O Allah, suffice me with what You have allowed instead of what You have forbidden, and make me independent of all others besides You.
items.note: A plea that Allah suffice you with what He has made lawful so you never need what He has forbidden, and free you of all need for anyone besides Him. The root of contentment with your provision.

items.title: Refuge from worry and debt
items.arabic: اللَّهُمَّ إِنِّي أَعُوذُ بِكَ مِنَ الْهَمِّ وَالْحَزَنِ، وَالْعَجْزِ وَالْكَسَلِ، وَالْبُخْلِ وَالْجُبْنِ، وَضَلَعِ الدَّيْنِ وَغَلَبَةِ الرِّجَالِ
items.translit: Allāhumma innī a`ūdhu bika mina ‘l-ḥammi wa ‘l-ḥazan, wa ‘l-`ajzi wa ‘l-kasal, wa ‘l-bukhli wa ‘l-jubn, wa ḍala`id-dayni wa ghalabatir-rijāl.
items.en: O Allah! I seek refuge with You from worry and grief, from incapacity and laziness, from cowardice and miserliness, from being heavily in debt and from being overpowered by (other) men.
items.note: The Prophet's ﷺ refuge from worry and grief, incapacity and laziness, miserliness and cowardice, and the two burdens that crush a person: heavy debt and being overpowered by others.

items.title: The du'a of the marketplace
items.arabic: لا إلهَ إلاّ اللّه وحدَهُ لا شريكَ لهُ، لهُ المُلْـكُ ولهُ الحَمْـد، يُحْيـي وَيُميـتُ وَهُوَ حَيٌّ لا يَمـوت، بِيَـدِهِ الْخَـيْرُ وَهوَ على كلّ شيءٍ قدير
items.translit: Lā ilāha illallāh waḥdahu lā sharīka lah, Lahu ‘l-mulku wa lahu ‘l-ḥamd, Yuḥyī wa yumīt, wa huwa ḥayyun lā yamūt, Biyadihi ‘l-khayr, wa huwa `alā kulli shay'in Qadīr.
items.en: None has the right to be worshipped but Allah alone, Who has no partner. His is the dominion and His is the praise. He brings life and He causes death, and He is living and does not die. In His Hand is all good, and He is Able to do all things.
items.note: Said as you enter the market. The Prophet ﷺ taught that whoever declares Allah's oneness here has a million good deeds written, a million sins erased, and is raised a million ranks; for the market is where hearts forget, and to remember Allah in it is precious.

before-sleep.name: Before Sleep
before-sleep.title: Before Sleep: the adhkar to say as you lie down, in Arabic, transliteration and meaning
before-sleep.meta_description: The adhkar before sleep (adhkar an-nawm): the three Quls, Ayat al-Kursi, the last verses of al-Baqarah, the tasbih of Fatimah and the last words of the night. Arabic, transliteration, meaning, source.
before-sleep.keywords: before sleep dua, adhkar before sleeping, dua before bed, Ayat al-Kursi before sleep, last two verses of Baqarah, tasbih before sleep 33, Hisn al-Muslim, Buruja
lede: You hand your soul back every night. The Prophet ﷺ called sleep the brother of death, and he never lay down without first placing the night, and himself, in the keeping of Allah.
These are the adhkar of sleep: protection breathed over the body, the verse that sets a guardian until morning, and the last words to say so that if you die that night, you die upon the fitrah.
intro: Say these as you get into bed. Begin with the three Quls breathed into your cupped hands and wiped over your body, then Ayat al-Kursi and the last two verses of al-Baqarah, your guardian and your sufficiency for the night.

Then the shorter words of lying down, the tasbih of thirty-three, and last of all the words of submission, said as you lie on your right side. The Prophet ﷺ told us to make them the very last thing we say, so that we fall asleep upon the remembrance of Allah.
before-sleep.closing_heading: Why we close the night this way
closing: Every night is a small rehearsal for the last one. You let go of the day, hand back your soul, and trust the One who keeps it whether or not you wake. The adhkar of sleep are how the believer lies down: protected, grateful, and ready, with His name as the last thing on the tongue.
O Allah, in Your name we lie down and in Your name we rise. If You take our souls tonight, have mercy on them; and if You return them, wake us to Your obedience.

faq.q: What should I recite before sleeping?
faq.a: Recite the three Quls into your cupped hands and wipe over your body, then Ayat al-Kursi and the last two verses of al-Baqarah. Add the shorter words of lying down and the tasbih of thirty-three, and end with the words of submission. All are gathered here with audio.

faq.q: Why recite Ayat al-Kursi before sleep?
faq.a: The Prophet ﷺ taught that whoever recites Ayat al-Kursi when lying down will have a guardian from Allah over them, and no devil will come near them until morning.

faq.q: What are the last words to say before sleeping?
faq.a: The words beginning 'Allahumma aslamtu nafsi ilayk' (O Allah, I submit myself to You...), said lying on the right side. The Prophet ﷺ said to make them the last thing you say, and that whoever dies that night dies upon the fitrah.

email.subject: How the believer lies down
email.alt_subject: The last words of the night
email.preview: The adhkar of sleep: protection breathed over the body, a guardian until morning.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

You hand your soul back every night. The Prophet ﷺ called sleep the brother of death, and he never lay down without first placing himself, and the night, in the keeping of Allah.

He would cup his hands, recite the three Quls into them, and wipe over his body. He taught that Ayat al-Kursi sets a guardian over you until morning, and that the last two verses of al-Baqarah suffice the one who recites them at night. And he told us to make the words of submission the very last thing we say, so that if we die in our sleep, we die upon the fitrah.

We have gathered the adhkar of sleep here, each with Arabic, transliteration, meaning and recitation, so you can close the night the way he did.

The full set is here:

social.hook:
Sleep is the brother of death.

The Prophet ﷺ never lay down without first handing the night back to Allah.
social.caption:
The adhkar before sleep.

The three Quls breathed into your hands and wiped over the body. Ayat al-Kursi, a guardian until morning. The last two verses of al-Baqarah. And the last words of the night, said on your right side, so that if you die before dawn, you die upon the fitrah.

Every night is a small rehearsal for the last one.

Full set with Arabic, transliteration, meaning and audio at buruja.com/adhkar/before-sleep
